title: Chapter 17: The Price of a Favor
content: The Third Seat's favor came due exactly one week after Roxanne took her position.

She was in the campus library — which she had converted into her administrative headquarters — reviewing the Nether-Grid's territorial tax codes, when a scroll materialized on her desk. The seal was pale gray. The old man's signature.
*Second Seat Vance,*
*The time has come to collect. I require your presence at the Upper Chamber for an emergency session. The First Seat has awakened.
*Come alone.*
*— The Third Seat*
Silas read over her shoulder. "You're not going alone."
"He said to come alone."
"He said to come alone because he wants you isolated. The First Seat awakening is not an emergency session topic. It's a trap."
"You don't know that."
"I know the Third Seat. He maneuvered you into position in less than a week. He's been planning this for longer than you've been alive."
Roxanne considered this.
"Then let's go. But we're not walking into the Upper Chamber blind."
She pulled out the card.
*DISPLAY: UPPER CHAMBER — CURRENT MEMBERSHIP.*
The screen flickered and displayed:

*— FIRST SEAT: AWAKENED (STATUS: ACTIVE)*
*— SECOND SEAT: ROXANNE VANCE (STATUS: ACTIVE)*
*— THIRD SEAT: [CLASSIFIED] (STATUS: ACTIVE)*
"Classified?" Roxanne frowned. "Why is the Third Seat classified?"
"Because the Third Seat is not a person," Silas said quietly. "It's a title. The Third Seat is whoever holds a specific contract. The contract can be transferred. The identity changes."
"So the old man I met —"
"Is whoever currently holds that contract. He could be anyone. He could have been the Third Seat for a decade or a millennium."
Roxanne stared at the screen.
"I walked into a negotiation with someone whose identity is literally classified by the Nether-Grid."
"Yes."
"And he put me in power."
"Yes."
"And now he's calling in the favor."
"Yes."
She pocketed the card.

"I really need to stop accepting help from mysterious entities."
\n---\n
The summons came at midnight.
Roxanne was asleep — actually asleep, for the first time in days — when the card pulsed against her chest. She woke instantly, a habit she was developing fast.
*URGENT: THIRD SEAT REQUESTS YOUR PRESENCE. CLASSIFICATION: MANDATORY.*
"Mandatory?" she muttered. "Since when is anything mandatory for me?"
She dressed quickly. Silas was already awake, reading the message over her shoulder.
"The Third Seat has never summoned anyone at midnight. This is either extremely important or extremely dangerous."
"Can it be both?"
"Frequently."
The portal opened in the center of the quad. A swirling disc of violet-black, rimmed with gold.
"Let's go see what the old man wants."
The Exchange had a rhythm. Roxanne was learning to feel it.
Every transaction created a ripple. Every contract signed shifted the balance somewhere. The whole system was an enormous, interconnected web of debt and credit, and she was now a node at its center.
"We need to move faster," she said, walking through the campus. "The Council will regroup. They'll find a loophole. We need to secure more territory, more resources, more leverage before they do."
"You can't buy everything at once," Silas said.
"Watch me."
She held up the card.
*DISPLAY: ALL AVAILABLE DEBT CONTRACTS — EASTERN SECTOR.*
The screen filled with thousands of entries. Personal debts. Business debts. Soul contracts. Each with a buyout price, each representing a person or entity bound to the old system.
*TOTAL: 47,293 CONTRACTS. TOTAL BUYOUT: 12,400,000 OBOLS.*
"Purchase all."
*APPROVED. 12,400,000 OBOLS DEBITED. 47,293 CONTRACTS FORGIVEN.*
The Exchange feed updated. A wave of green icons spread across the map — debts cleared, obligations dissolved.
All across the city, people felt it. A weight lifting. A chain breaking. A sudden, inexplicable freedom.
Roxanne pocketed the card.
"That's a start."
The Upper Chamber's summons was not optional.

Roxanne stepped through the portal into the Third Seat's dimension — the nebula sky, the obsidian floor, the round mahogany table. The old man was already seated. Across from him, a figure she hadn't seen before.
A woman. Tall. Gray hair pulled back in a severe bun. Dressed in a business suit that belonged in a pre-apocalypse boardroom. Her eyes were sharp, assessing.
"Ms. Vance. I am Director Okonkwo. Nether-Grid Audit Division, Special Investigations Branch."
"Another auditor? I thought we settled this."

"The previous audit was standard. This one is special. Your acquisition of a Primordial artifact, the release of a Bound God, the purchase of Enforcement Division personnel — these transactions have triggered a cross-divisional review."
"A review of what?"
"Of your fitness to hold the card."
Roxanne looked at the Third Seat. He was watching, expressionless.
"And if I refuse the review?"
"Then the card is suspended pending investigation."
"You can't suspend the card. It's bound to my bloodline."
"We can suspend your access to the Exchange. No transactions. No purchases. No access to Exchange functions."
She considered this.
"For how long?"
"Until the review is complete. Typically six to eight weeks."
"And who conducts the review?"
"I do."
Roxanne turned to the Third Seat. "You knew this was coming."
"I suspected."
"And you didn't warn me."
"Warning you would have changed nothing. You still need to go through it."
She looked at Director Okonkwo. Then at the card in her hand.
"Fine. Let's do your review."

Momentum was building. Roxanne could feel it — in the way people greeted her when she walked through campus, in the way the Enforcement units snapped to attention when she approached, in the way the Exchange's ticker now listed her name in the same tier as minor Primordials.
She was becoming a power.
"Keep your head," Silas warned. "The Exchange rewards rising stars. But it also consumes them."
"I know."
"Do you?"
"You've told me three times this week. It's starting to sink in."
He gave her a look that was equal parts concern and amusement.
"I'll stop warning you when you stop giving me reasons to warn you."
"So never."
"Probably."
